title: ECG Interpretation: Axis, Ischemia, Hypertrophy & Rhythm Quiz

questions.text: The mean QRS axis represents:

answers.text: The total duration of ventricular depolarization
The average direction of depolarization that spreads through the ventricles
The average heart rate over a 6-second window
The angle of the heart within the chest cavity

questions.text: How many degrees separate each lead in the hexaxial reference system?
What is the normal mean QRS axis range?
Left axis deviation is defined by which range?
Using the quick-look method, lead I is positive and lead aVF is negative. The axis is:
Which of the following is most likely to cause RIGHT axis deviation?
Which of the following is most likely to cause LEFT axis deviation?
Leads II, III, and aVF examine tissue supplied by which coronary artery?
ST segment elevation is defined as elevation equal to or greater than 1 mm above the isoelectric line at a point how far to the right of the J point, in 3 or more consecutive leads?
In the hyperacute phase of a STEMI, the ECG most characteristically shows:

answers.text: Down-sloping ST elevation with deep Q waves
A T wave measuring more than 50% of the preceding R wave
ST depression with T wave inversion

questions.text: The first ECG sign that myocardial tissue death has occurred (fully evolved STEMI) is:

answers.text: Up-sloping ST elevation with a tall T wave
Deep, abnormal Q waves with down-sloping ST elevation and T wave inversion
ST depression in the inferior leads

questions.text: How is NSTEMI diagnosed when the ECG shows no ST elevation?

answers.text: By the presence of axis deviation alone
By signs and symptoms, history, and cardiac biomarker results

questions.text: Significant ST depression in leads V2 and V3 is defined as a depression of more than:
Which leads view the SEPTAL wall of the left ventricle?
Which leads view the INFERIOR wall of the left ventricle?
Posterior wall infarction requires additional chest leads placed further left and toward the back. These leads are:
The clinical triad of right ventricular infarction is:

answers.text: Hypotension, jugular venous distention, clear breath sounds

questions.text: Right atrial enlargement most characteristically produces:

answers.text: A widened, notched P wave > 0.11 seconds
An abnormally tall initial part of the P wave; biphasic P in V1

questions.text: Left atrial enlargement is recognized by:

answers.text: A tall initial portion of the P wave
A widened latter portion of the P wave > 0.11 seconds, often notched in I, II, aVL, V4–V6
An inverted P wave in lead II

questions.text: What is the voltage criterion for suspecting LVH?

answers.text: S wave in V1 + R wave in V5 ≥ 35 mV
T wave amplitude > 5 mm in V5

questions.text: One of the earliest and most reliable ECG findings of right ventricular hypertrophy is:
Sodium imbalances (hypernatremia or hyponatremia) typically cause which ECG change?
Hyperkalemia most characteristically produces which ECG findings?

answers.text: Wide QRS complexes and prolonged PR intervals
Long, flat ST segments and prolonged QT
Tall, narrow QRS complexes with short PR intervals

questions.text: Hypocalcemia most characteristically produces which ECG findings?

answers.text: Long, flat ST segments and prolonged QT interval

questions.text: Using the small box method on a regular rhythm, you count 20 small boxes between R-R. The ventricular rate is:
